---
title: "From Survey Data to Behavioural ABMs"
subtitle: "Evaluating the Limits of LLM Agents in Preventive Behaviour Modelling"
author: "Adam Amer"
institute: "<span class='title-slide-degree'>MSc in Artificial Intelligence · Bocconi University</span><span class='title-slide-advisors'><span class='title-slide-meta-line'><strong>Supervisor:</strong> Prof. Alessia Melegaro</span><span class='title-slide-meta-line'><strong>Advisor:</strong> Lorenzo Lucchini</span></span>"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [simple, custom.scss]
    slide-number: true
    progress: true
    hash: true
    navigation-mode: linear
    controls: true
    center: false
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    margin: 0.05
    transition: none
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-color: "#fafbfc"
    mermaid:
      theme: neutral
    self-contained: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

## Research motivation: Policy needs counterfactuals {.smaller}

**Core challenge**

- Policymakers need to reason about alternative interventions
- Social systems are hard to randomise cleanly
- Outcomes depend on **heterogeneity, interaction, and feedback**

<br>

**Possible solution: ABMs**

- They provide a computational laboratory for counterfactual policy analysis
- But aggregate fit alone does not identify the right **micro-mechanism**

<br>

**LLMs as the micro-mechanism?**

- They promise richer behavioural rules
- But that promise must be tested

::: {.notes}
Policy design is fundamentally counterfactual. In social sciences, clean experiments are rare. ABMs can move this logic into computation, but they face the identification problem: aggregate fit doesn't guarantee correct micro-mechanisms.
:::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 3: THESIS PROPOSAL - PIPELINE -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Thesis Proposal: From empirics to simulation {.pipeline-slide}

```{=html}
<div class="pipeline-grid">
  <div class="pipeline-card pipeline-card-blue">
    <div class="pipeline-title">1. Empirical backbone</div>
    <div class="pipeline-body">survey micro-data<br>→ theory-guided blocks<br>→ reference equations</div>
  </div>

  <div class="pipeline-arrow">→</div>

  <div class="pipeline-card pipeline-card-mid">
    <div class="pipeline-title">2. LLM micro-validation</div>
    <div class="pipeline-body">• baseline reconstruction<br>• perturbed response tests</div>
  </div>

  <div class="pipeline-arrow">→</div>

  <div class="pipeline-card pipeline-card-light">
    <div class="pipeline-title">3. Survey-grounded ABM</div>
    <div class="pipeline-body">• social interaction<br>• epidemic feedback<br>→ policy comparison</div>
  </div>
</div>
```


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 4: EMPIRICAL BACKBONE - DATA -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Empirical backbone: Data and outcomes {.backbone-slide}

```{=html}
<div class="backbone-grid">
  <div class="backbone-panel">
    <div class="backbone-panel-title">Survey base</div>
    <div class="backbone-structured-text">
      <div class="backbone-text-section">
        <div class="backbone-text-label">Sample</div>
        <div class="backbone-text-content">~22,000 respondents · 6 European countries · 2024 survey wave</div>
      </div>
      <div class="backbone-text-section">
        <div class="backbone-text-label">Countries</div>
        <div class="backbone-text-content">Italy, Spain, France, Germany, Hungary, UK</div>
      </div>
      <div class="backbone-text-section">
        <div class="backbone-text-label">Survey domains</div>
        <div class="backbone-text-content">Demographics, health, vaccine beliefs, information environment, social exposure, trust, barriers, moral orientation</div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <div class="backbone-panel">
    <div class="backbone-panel-title">Core prevention outcomes</div>
    <table class="backbone-outcomes-table">
      <thead>
        <tr>
          <th>Outcome</th>
          <th>Description</th>
        </tr>
      </thead>
      <tbody>
        <tr>
          <td>COVID vaccination willingness</td>
          <td>first/second dose phase</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>Flu vaccination</td>
          <td>2023/2024 uptake</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td>NPI behaviours</td>
          <td>
            <ul class="backbone-compact-list">
              <li>masking when symptomatic</li>
              <li>staying home when symptomatic</li>
              <li>masking under high epidemic pressure</li>
            </ul>
          </td>
        </tr>
      </tbody>
    </table>
  </div>
</div>
```


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 5: EMPIRICAL BACKBONE - METHOD -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## From high-dimensional survey to compact backbone

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Reduction pipeline**

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 6
%%| fig-height: 4.5
flowchart TD
    A[Raw survey items<br/>~100+ questions] --> B[Derived variables<br/>& indices]
    B --> C[Theory-guided<br/>blocks]
    C --> D[Fitted equations<br/>per outcome]
    D --> E[Retained backbone<br/>unique signal only]
    
    style A fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#5d6d7e
    style E fill:#e8f1f8,stroke:#1e3a5f
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Theory-guided blocks**

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

[Stakes]{.block-tag}
[Trust]{.block-tag}
[Legitimacy]{.block-tag}
[Disposition]{.block-tag}

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

[Habit]{.block-tag}
[Moral orientation]{.block-tag}
[Constraints]{.block-tag}
[Norms]{.block-tag}

:::

::::

<br>

**Goal:** A compact, interpretable representation that:

- Carries into LLM evaluation
- Anchors the ABM behavioural core
- Identifies unique signal per block

:::

::::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 6: EMPIRICAL RESULTS - BLOCK IMPORTANCE -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Prevention has a compact but outcome-specific structure

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="45%"}

**Key finding:**

The same broad blocks recur, but their **relative importance changes** by outcome

<br>

**COVID vaccination**

- Dominant: [Stakes]{.block-tag}
- Supporting: [Trust]{.block-tag} [Legitimacy]{.block-tag} [Disposition]{.block-tag}

<br>

**Flu vaccination**

- Dominant: [Habit]{.block-tag}
- Without habit: age + health become central

<br>

**Non-pharmaceutical (NPIs)**

- Dominant: [Moral orientation]{.block-tag}
- Supporting: [Stakes]{.block-tag}

:::

::: {.column width="55%"}

**Block importance by outcome**

<div style="font-size: 0.75em;">

| Block | COVID Vax | Flu Vax | NPIs |
|:------|:---------:|:-------:|:----:|
| **Stakes** | ████ | █ | ██ |
| **Trust** | ██ | █ | █ |
| **Legitimacy** | ██ | · | · |
| **Disposition** | ██ | █ | █ |
| **Habit** | █ | ████ | · |
| **Moral orient.** | · | · | ███ |

</div>

::: {.small}
████ = dominant · ██/███ = important · █ = present · · = negligible
:::

:::

::::

::: {.takeaway}
Same framework, different weights — behaviour-specific policy levers
:::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 7: EMPIRICAL RESULTS - PERTURBATION -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Which levers actually move behaviour?

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Reference perturbation effect**

Move one variable from typical low → typical high, holding rest fixed

<br>

**Results align with block importance:**

| Outcome | Strongest lever |
|---------|-----------------|
| COVID vaccination | Stakes, Disposition, Trust |
| Flu vaccination | Limited short-run leverage (habit-driven) |
| NPIs | Dispersed effects (headroom matters) |

<br>

::: {.concept-box}
**Headroom effect:** Profiles already near ceiling/floor show limited response to shifts
:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 6.5
%%| fig-height: 5
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["Perturbation Design"]
        direction LR
        LOW[Low profile<br/>value] -->|"Shift"| HIGH[High profile<br/>value]
    end
    
    subgraph Model["Fitted Equation"]
        EQ[Predict outcome<br/>change]
    end
    
    subgraph Result["Reference Effect"]
        direction TB
        R1["Stakes → COVID vax: ↑↑↑"]
        R2["Habit → Flu vax: ↑ (inertial)"]
        R3["Moral → NPIs: ↑↑"]
    end
    
    Input --> Model --> Result
    
    style Input fill:#e8f1f8,stroke:#1e3a5f
    style Model fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#5d6d7e
    style Result fill:#eef5ed,stroke:#27ae60
```

:::

::::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 8: LLM EVALUATION - DESIGN -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## LLM micro-validation: Testing against the empirical benchmark

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}

**Evaluation design:**

| Setting | Question |
|---------|----------|
| **Unperturbed** | Can LLM reconstruct baseline survey patterns? |
| **Perturbed** | Does LLM respond correctly to controlled shifts? |

<br>

**Two accuracy dimensions:**

1. **Level accuracy** — Predict correct value
2. **Rank agreement** — Order respondents correctly (low → high propensity)

<br>

**Applied to:**

- Behavioural outcomes (vaccination, NPIs)
- Psychological profile variables (state dimensions for simulation)

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5.5
%%| fig-height: 5
flowchart TD
    subgraph Benchmark["Empirical Benchmark"]
        E1[Survey-grounded<br/>patterns]
    end
    
    subgraph Tests["Evaluation Matrix"]
        T1[Unperturbed<br/>baseline]
        T2[Perturbed<br/>shifts]
    end
    
    subgraph Metrics["Assessment"]
        M1[Level<br/>accuracy]
        M2[Rank<br/>agreement]
    end
    
    subgraph Targets["Variables"]
        V1[Behavioural<br/>outcomes]
        V2[Profile<br/>states]
    end
    
    Benchmark --> Tests
    Tests --> Metrics
    Metrics --> Targets
    
    style Benchmark fill:#e8f1f8,stroke:#1e3a5f
    style Tests fill:#f8f9fa,stroke:#5d6d7e
    style Targets fill:#eef5ed,stroke:#27ae60
```

:::

::::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 9: LLM RESULTS - BASELINE -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Baseline structure is partly recovered

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Mixed but non-trivial results:**

<br>

::: {.result-box}
**✓ Ranking** — LLMs can order respondents from lower to higher propensity
:::

<br>

::: {.result-box}
**✗ Level accuracy** — Predictions compress toward the middle
:::

<br>

**Central-tendency compression:**

- Low values overpredicted
- High values underpredicted
- Heterogeneity reduced

<br>

::: {.muted}
Same pattern appears for psychological profile variables — would make simulated agents too similar
:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Baseline performance pattern**

<div style="display: flex; align-items: flex-end; height: 200px; gap: 60px; justify-content: center; margin-top: 40px;">
<div style="text-align: center;">
<div style="background: linear-gradient(to top, #1e3a5f 0%, #4a90c2 100%); width: 80px; height: 130px; border-radius: 4px 4px 0 0;"></div>
<div style="margin-top: 10px; font-size: 0.8em; color: #5d6d7e;">Ranking<br/>✓ Moderate</div>
</div>
<div style="text-align: center;">
<div style="background: linear-gradient(to top, #95a5a6 0%, #bdc3c7 100%); width: 80px; height: 80px; border-radius: 4px 4px 0 0;"></div>
<div style="margin-top: 10px; font-size: 0.8em; color: #5d6d7e;">Level<br/>✗ Weak</div>
</div>
</div>

<br>

::: {.concept-box}
**Implication:** LLMs are better at *relative* ordering than *absolute* positioning
:::

:::

::::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 10: LLM RESULTS - COUNTERFACTUAL BREAKDOWN -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Counterfactual coherence breaks down

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}

**Under controlled perturbations:**

- Responses often too weak or too strong
- Effects too diffuse across respondents
- Paired comparisons help somewhat
- **Effect sizes remain miscalibrated**

<br>

**Core problem:**

LLMs spread effects too widely instead of preserving the selective structure from the empirical benchmark

<br>

::: {.takeaway}
Off-the-shelf LLMs are not reliable as behavioural update mechanisms for counterfactual ABMs
:::

:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5.5
%%| fig-height: 5.5
flowchart TD
    subgraph Baseline["Baseline"]
        B[Moderate<br/>ranking recovery]
    end
    
    subgraph Perturbed["Under Perturbation"]
        P1[Too weak<br/>or too strong]
        P2[Too diffuse<br/>across profiles]
        P3[Effect sizes<br/>miscalibrated]
    end
    
    subgraph Boundary["Clear Boundary"]
        X[❌ Not suitable for<br/>counterfactual ABM]
    end
    
    Baseline --> Perturbed
    Perturbed --> Boundary
    
    style Baseline fill:#eef5ed,stroke:#27ae60
    style Perturbed fill:#fff5f5,stroke:#c0392b
    style Boundary fill:#fff5f5,stroke:#c0392b
```

:::

::::

::: {.notes}
This is one of the key contribution slides. The boundary is clear: baseline plausibility is not enough for simulation.
:::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 11: ABM ARCHITECTURE -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## ABM architecture: Survey-grounded behavioural core

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 14
%%| fig-height: 6
flowchart LR
    subgraph Core["Behavioural Core"]
        direction TB
        SURVEY[Survey-grounded<br/>equations] --> TARGETS[Time-varying<br/>targets]
        TARGETS --> PARTIAL[Partial<br/>adjustment]
    end
    
    subgraph Dynamics["Dynamic Mechanisms"]
        direction TB
        PEER[Peer exposure<br/>homophily-weighted]
        NORM[Norm updating<br/>threshold contagion]
        SOCIAL[Social<br/>interaction]
        PEER --> SOCIAL
        NORM --> SOCIAL
    end
    
    subgraph Feedback["Epidemic Feedback"]
        direction TB
        INC[Local incidence<br/>signal]
        PROTECT[Protection<br/>dampens spread]
        RISK[Feeds back to<br/>perceived risk]
        INC --> PROTECT
        PROTECT --> RISK
    end
    
    Core --> Dynamics
    Dynamics --> Feedback
    Feedback --> Core
    
    style Core fill:#e8f1f8,stroke:#1e3a5f
    style Dynamics fill:#eef5ed,stroke:#27ae60
    style Feedback fill:#fef5e7,stroke:#d68910
```

<br>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Partial adjustment**

States move gradually toward targets; trust/norms adjust slowly, risk appraisals faster
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Social influence**

Peer-pulling on trust/risk; norm updating via threshold visibility
:::

::: {.column width="33%"}
**Incidence loop**

Higher protection → lower incidence → lower perceived risk
:::

::::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 12: ABM INTERVENTIONS -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Interventions reveal distinct dynamic profiles

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Four intervention types compared:**

| Type | Duration | Mechanism |
|------|----------|-----------|
| **Norm campaigns** | 9 months | Social reinforcement |
| **Credibility repair** | 9 months | Trust/legitimacy |
| **Outbreak wave** | Pulse | Risk salience |
| **Access facilitation** | 9 months | Barrier removal |

<br>

**Key findings:**

::: {.result-box}
**Norms** → Largest cumulative effects (persist through social reinforcement)
:::

::: {.result-box}
**Outbreak** → Sharp but short-lived (relaxes when pressure declines)
:::

::: {.result-box}
**Credibility** → Asymmetric spillovers (legitimacy ≠ trust)
:::

::: {.result-box}
**Access** → Substitution effect (↑ vaccination → ↓ incidence → ↓ other precautions)
:::

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Cumulative effect profiles**

<div style="display: flex; align-items: flex-end; height: 220px; gap: 30px; justify-content: center; margin-top: 20px;">
<div style="text-align: center;">
<div style="background: linear-gradient(to top, #1e3a5f 0%, #2d5a87 100%); width: 65px; height: 180px; border-radius: 4px 4px 0 0;"></div>
<div style="margin-top: 8px; font-size: 0.7em; color: #5d6d7e;">Norms</div>
</div>
<div style="text-align: center;">
<div style="background: linear-gradient(to top, #2d5a87 0%, #4a90c2 100%); width: 65px; height: 120px; border-radius: 4px 4px 0 0;"></div>
<div style="margin-top: 8px; font-size: 0.7em; color: #5d6d7e;">Credibility</div>
</div>
<div style="text-align: center;">
<div style="background: linear-gradient(to top, #4a90c2 0%, #6ba3d6 100%); width: 65px; height: 75px; border-radius: 4px 4px 0 0;"></div>
<div style="margin-top: 8px; font-size: 0.7em; color: #5d6d7e;">Outbreak</div>
</div>
<div style="text-align: center;">
<div style="background: linear-gradient(to top, #95a5a6 0%, #bdc3c7 100%); width: 65px; height: 35px; border-radius: 4px 4px 0 0;"></div>
<div style="margin-top: 8px; font-size: 0.7em; color: #5d6d7e;">Access</div>
</div>
</div>

<br>

::: {.concept-box}
**Substitution insight:** Improving one prevention margin does not necessarily raise prevention everywhere
:::

:::

::::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 13: ABM SENSITIVITY -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Sensitivity analysis: Where do effects come from?

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Morris screening by mechanism family:**

| Family | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Persistence** | How slowly states relax |
| **Social diffusion** | Network propagation strength |
| **Incidence feedback** | Behaviour ↔ epidemic loop |
| **Stochasticity** | Random variation |

<br>

**Interpretable pattern:**

- **Credibility** → most sensitive to *persistence*
- **Norms** → most sensitive to *social diffusion*
- **Access** → sensitive to *stochasticity* + *incidence feedback*

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Sensitivity by mechanism family**

<div style="font-size: 0.75em;">

| Intervention | Persistence | Diffusion | Incidence | Stochastic |
|:-------------|:-----------:|:---------:|:---------:|:----------:|
| **Credibility** | ███ | █ | █ | █ |
| **Norms** | █ | ███ | █ | █ |
| **Access** | █ | █ | ██ | ██ |

</div>

::: {.small}
███ = primary driver · ██ = important · █ = minor
:::

<br>

::: {.concept-box}
**Robustness:** Cumulative rankings often more robust than month-12 rankings (mean-reverting dynamics)
:::

:::

::::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- SLIDE 14: TAKEAWAYS -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Takeaways

<br>

::: {.takeaway style="font-size: 1.2em; margin-bottom: 1.5em;"}
**1.** Prevention behaviour has a **compact but outcome-specific** empirical structure — same blocks, different weights
:::

::: {.takeaway style="font-size: 1.2em; margin-bottom: 1.5em;"}
**2.** Off-the-shelf LLMs recover some baseline patterns but are **not counterfactually disciplined** for behavioural updating in simulation
:::

::: {.takeaway style="font-size: 1.2em; margin-bottom: 1.5em;"}
**3.** A **survey-grounded hybrid pipeline** — empirical backbone + dynamic mechanisms — offers a more credible route to policy simulation
:::

<br>
<br>

::: {style="text-align: center; color: #5d6d7e; font-size: 0.9em;"}
**Thank you**

Questions?
:::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- BACKUP SLIDES -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## {.center background-color="#1e3a5f"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
**Backup Slides**
:::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- BACKUP: BLOCK DICTIONARY -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Construct / block dictionary {.smaller}

::: {.backup-marker}
BACKUP
:::

| Block | Components | Description |
|-------|------------|-------------|
| **Stakes** | Perceived danger, fear, infection likelihood, vulnerability | How threatened does the person feel? |
| **Institutional Trust** | Trust in government, health authorities, science | Confidence in public institutions |
| **Legitimacy** | Acceptance of vaccine rollout, governance process | Was the policy process fair? |
| **Disposition** | Vaccine confidence, general attitudes | Pre-existing stance toward vaccines |
| **Habit** | Past vaccination behaviour | Routine vs. deliberative decision |
| **Moral Orientation** | Individualising vs. binding foundations | Prosocial vs. authority-based framing |
| **Norms** | Perceived prevalence, social expectations | What do others do and expect? |
| **Constraints** | Access barriers, time, information | Practical obstacles |


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- BACKUP: LLM EVALUATION DETAILS -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## LLM evaluation: Prompt strategies {.smaller}

::: {.backup-marker}
BACKUP
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Prompting variants tested:**

- Zero-shot description
- Profile-with-demographics
- Chain-of-thought reasoning
- Paired comparison (explicit contrast)

<br>

**Models evaluated:**

- GPT-4 / GPT-3.5
- Claude variants
- Open-source alternatives

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Metrics:**

| Metric | Measures |
|--------|----------|
| MAE | Level accuracy |
| Spearman ρ | Rank agreement |
| Calibration slope | Compression degree |
| Paired accuracy | Contrast discrimination |

<br>

::: {.muted}
Full results in appendix
:::

:::

::::


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- BACKUP: ABM EQUATIONS -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## ABM equations: Partial adjustment {.smaller}

::: {.backup-marker}
BACKUP
:::

**State update:**

$$
s_{i,u}(t+1) = s_{i,u}(t) + \kappa_u\big(\tilde s_{i,u}(t)-s_{i,u}(t)\big)
$$

where $\kappa_u \in \{k_{\text{fast}}, k_{\text{slow}}\}$

<br>

**Target composition:**

$$
\tilde s_{i,u}(t) = s_{i,u}^{\text{base}} + \Delta^{\text{policy}}(t) + \Delta^{\text{social}}(t) + \Delta^{\text{incidence}}(t)
$$

<br>

**Norm updating (threshold):**

$$
\text{norm}_{i}(t+1) = \text{norm}_{i}(t) + \alpha_i \cdot \mathbb{1}\left[\text{observed} > \theta_i\right] \cdot g(\text{observed})
$$


<!-- ============================================= -->
<!-- BACKUP: MORRIS SENSITIVITY -->
<!-- ============================================= -->

## Morris sensitivity: Full results {.smaller}

::: {.backup-marker}
BACKUP
:::

**Elementary effects screening:**

- Parameter space partitioned into mechanism families
- μ* captures average absolute effect
- σ captures interaction/non-linearity

<br>

**Key insights:**

1. Credibility interventions → persistence parameters dominate
2. Norm interventions → social diffusion parameters dominate
3. Access interventions → mixed (incidence + stochastic)
4. Cumulative AUC rankings more stable than point-in-time rankings

::: {.muted}
Confirms that intervention types work through distinct mechanisms, and that model structure drives the differentiation
:::
